blocks.content: Julian had watched Davenport's car leave from his study window.

He'd been up since five-thirty. The legal pad was on its fourth page. He'd been mapping it — not the events, those were clear enough. The people. Their positions relative to each other. What each person gained and lost from various outcomes.
Gideon Price. Davenport called at seven this morning. Told me the name. Told me what Price's record looked like. Asked what I thought.

I told him Price is a real thread but the wrong thread. A man like Price doesn't use sleeping pills and piano wire. He uses fear. He uses people who show up at your door. He doesn't plan for months and execute through a locked window.

But Julian understood why Davenport had to pursue it. Because if Price was responsible, the case was clean. External actor, clear motive, known criminal record. The five people in the house next door went home. Everyone moved on.

Davenport wants Price to be the answer. Not because he's lazy — because he's decent. Because the alternative means one of five people he's spent two days watching cry and grieve over their friend is performing it.
Julian turned back to his page.
He had drawn six circles. One was crossed out — Marcus. The remaining five had lines between them, weights attached, histories noted in shorthand.
He looked at the circle he kept coming back to. Didn't write the name.
